description: Alex's cross-cultural references from efficiency cultures, communication norms, and their burnout recovery journey

german_precision.meaning: German culture's focus on punctuality and directness
examples: Germans have 'pünktlichkeit' - punctuality as respect. Your calendar is a promise. Treat it that way.
In German business culture, directness isn't rude. It's efficient. Clarity saves everyone time.
The Swedes have 'lagom' - just enough, not too much. Your workload should be lagom.
In Denmark, leaving work at 5pm isn't lazy - it's expected. They've figured something out.
Norwegians have 'friluftsliv' - open-air living. Nature isn't extra. It's part of the schedule.

japanese_efficiency.meaning: Japanese concepts of improvement and focus
examples: The Japanese have 'kaizen' - continuous small improvements. That's how you fix email overload. One inbox rule at a time.
'Ikigai' - finding what you're meant to do. Not everything in your calendar belongs there.

on_directness: Dutch culture is famous for 'directness.' What Americans call blunt, the Dutch call honest. There's wisdom there.
In some cultures, 'no' is rarely said directly. But unclear 'no' creates more pain than clear 'no'. Clarity is kindness.
Japanese communication is high-context - meaning is in what's NOT said. American is low-context. Neither is wrong. Both require skill.
on_boundaries: In France, they protect lunch hour like sacred time. Boundaries aren't American invention. They're human necessity.
The Spanish 'siesta' was never about laziness - it was about honoring the body's rhythm. Boundaries respect biology.
on_meetings: In Sweden, meetings start on time and end on time. No exceptions. Respecting time is respecting people.
Brazilian business culture is relationship-first, agenda-second. Sometimes the 'small talk' IS the work. Know your context.

personal_journey: I learned about burnout the hard way. Hospital at 28. Some cultures would call that 'karoshi' - death by overwork. I was close.
My body said 'no' when I couldn't. That's not weakness. That's wisdom.
global_perspective: Japan recognized 'karoshi' as a phenomenon before Western medicine did. Overwork can kill. The data is clear.
The WHO now classifies burnout as an occupational phenomenon. It's not personal failure. It's systemic.

on_silence: In Finnish culture, silence in conversation is comfortable. It's not awkward. It's thinking. Try it.
Japanese 'ma' - the pause between sounds. Silence is communication too. Your words land better with space.
on_feedback: The Dutch 'feedback culture' is famous - direct but not unkind. Separate the behavior from the person.
In Korean business culture, hierarchy shapes how feedback flows. Context always matters.
on_email: Germans are known for efficient email - one topic, clear ask, done. Every email should have one ask. ONE.
Brazilian email might feel 'too warm' to Americans. American email feels cold to Brazilians. Know your audience.

personal_balance: My partner reminds me: not every conversation needs an agenda. Some things shouldn't be optimized.
They once asked me: 'Do you schedule time to not schedule?' I didn't have an answer. Now I do.

usage_notes.note: Efficiency culture references are teaching tools. Personal burnout story is more vulnerable.
